ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301103 represents tea beverage as a category; no specific code exists for house-made warm tea. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN0KL covers tea infusion broadly; no specific code for house-made warm tea. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 refers to tea as a substance; used as closest parent for this brewed tea beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (approximately 240 ml / 240 g) of warm house-made brewed tea, as typically served at a café or casual dining restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for brewed black tea prepared with tap water (NDB No. 14355 / FDC ID 171917). House-made tea is assumed to be a plain brewed tea without added milk or sugar, consistent with this reference. Values are per 100 g of brewed tea liquid.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — plain tea and zero-carbohydrate beverages convention
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates (carbohydrate_g = 0.0 per 100 g). No measurable GI can be determined for a zero-carbohydrate beverage. By convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages (consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance for plain tea and black coffee), glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value), gi_category is set to 'low', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. This is consistent with peer-reviewed literature confirming plain tea has negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Plain brewed tea without added milk or sugar contains negligible sodium (approximately 7 mg per 240 ml serving) and is considered safe and beneficial for individuals managing hypertension; regular tea consumption has been associated with modest reductions in blood pressure in epidemiological studies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycemic carbohydrates; negligible caloric load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain house-made tea contains no fermentable carbohydrates and has a glycemic load of zero per serving, making it an appropriate beverage choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; polyphenols in tea may additionally support insulin sensitivity when consumed without added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of tea polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain brewed tea is free of saturated fat and cholesterol and contains flavonoids and catechins that have been associated with reduced LDL oxidation and improved endothelial function, supporting cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Warm House-made Tea is a plain hot brewed tea beverage served at Flavours by Saute, a café-style restaurant located in Funan Mall, Singapore. The tea is prepared in-house by steeping tea leaves in hot water, yielding a simple, lightly flavoured, aromatic beverage with no added milk or sugar in its base form. The flavour profile is clean, mildly astringent, and warming, characteristic of a straightforward brewed tea. Nutritionally, plain brewed tea is essentially a zero-calorie, zero-macronutrient beverage: it contains approximately 1 kcal per 100 g, with no protein, no fat, and no carbohydrates, making it one of the most nutritionally neutral beverages available. It provides trace amounts of potassium (37 mg per 100 g), magnesium (3 mg per 100 g), and naturally occurring fluoride. Tea polyphenols, including catechins and flavonoids, are present and have been associated with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cardioprotective effects in peer-reviewed literature. The glycemic index is effectively zero (set to 1 by convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages), and the glycemic load per serving is 0.0, making it entirely appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free. In the Singapore food context, house-made tea is a common accompaniment to meals across all cuisine types and is widely consumed as a standalone beverage throughout the day.
common_user_queries: Is house-made tea good for diabetics?
How many calories in warm tea?
Is plain tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of brewed tea?
Nutritional value of plain brewed tea

confidence_reason: The food is described as 'house-made tea' without specification of tea type (black, green, oolong, herbal). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plain brewed black tea (NDB 14355), which is the most common reference for generic brewed tea. The adaptation is defensible as all plain brewed teas share essentially the same near-zero macronutrient profile. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact tea variety and preparation method at Flavours by Saute are not publicly documented in an approved source.
notes: 1. energy_kcal is 1.0 kcal per 100 g, reflecting the trace caloric content of plain brewed tea per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14355). 2. protein_g is naturally zero — plain brewed tea contains no protein by nature. 3. fat_g is naturally zero — plain brewed tea contains no fat by nature. 4. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — plain brewed tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates by nature; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 5. sugar_g is naturally zero — no sugars present in plain brewed tea without added sweeteners. 6. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero — brewed tea liquid contains no dietary fibre. 7. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are set to 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA source for plain brewed tea. 9. potassium_mg (37.0), magnesium_mg (3.0), phosphorus_mg (1.0), zinc_mg (0.02), iron_mg (0.02), and vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.01) are reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 14355. 10. All remaining micronutrients set to 0.0 are confirmed zero or below detection threshold per USDA source. 11. The tea type (black, green, oolong, herbal) is not specified in the field survey listing; values reflect generic plain brewed tea. 12. Price SGD 3.50 preserved from input. 13. Cuisine tags from input (Ramen, Pizza, Pasta) reflect the restaurant's broader menu and are not applicable to this beverage item.

Set glycemic_index to 1 (sentinel) and gi_category to 'low' per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule.
Documented glycemic_index sentinel value in notes and gi_source.

Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases.

All per_100g values are numeric; no -1 sentinel values used (all nutrients reported from USDA source); documented in notes.
